item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How does no one know this is just a reference to the Rush song “Distant Early Warning”

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image used here is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it's licensed under Public Domain as indicated here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a very well built article in my opinion for which I upvoted it… and tried to re-upvote when I got to the translation of the message. So I might not have read all the articles in existence, not even a majority of them, but I found so many that treat translation like some kind of weird magic that "sorts of happens". There are people who think that if they add some "unsure" words in their "translation" it will validate the fact that the Foundation linguists just pulled out of their asses a translation of an alien or forgotten language without any base for comparison.

That's why I'm more than happy that the author of this article treated translations for what they are and not what they are wished to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The base-12 number works out to around 1.1 million years, so unless it got reset at some point, it would seem to be off by a factor of 10. Is that a mistake?

item.title: Well done
item.content:encoded:
Well done on that scp you have my +1. The minor thing is, Class E amnesiacs are, according to the wiki, to be given through IV, not as an Aerial dispense system. You'd need Class A for that.
Other than that, well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I couldn't tell you exactly why, but something about this is incredibly creepy. Especially some of the swarm redirecting themselves to the lone one's broadcast. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I went and dug up the Leak post for this one1. It doesn't explain too much about the broadcasts directly, but it does confirm that the language is partly based on Hebrew and Celtic.
Running words through google translate and throwing in my own love of root language studies gets some interesting results-

  • "Tevas" is similar to a few different Hebrew terms for "angry"- assuming a Celtic system of modifiers, Be-Tevas could be "very angry". My guess is that it's a doomsday clock-type threat level prediction.
  • "Hemachem" is similar to both a modern Hebrew word for radar and a set of words meaning variants on "from you", while "am Mollach" could be "opposite/behind you". Not sure what to take from that. Maybe a relative-distance-to-threat range or retaliation window or something?
  • Reham is similar to "smell/sense", "coming from", "rise from"… My guess is a direction-to-threat or "threat type".
Footnotes
1. the revelations are mild for this one, and actually open up some more interesting lines of thought, especially juxtaposed with some more recent additions to site lore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In recordings in 1945, 1947-1973, 1979, 1983, 1997-1998, 2005, and 2009-2011, the phrase "Bé-tevàs" is heard instead.

Didn't want to reply to them directly because boo necroposting, but like Lee and Meserach I'm very curious to what these are. In some cases it seems to relate to events (like the atomic bombs beign dropped in 1945 and the Three Mile Island Accident mentioned before), but a lot just seem to refer to global attitudes and events regarding nuclear technology at the time but not actual nuclear incidents themselves (Cold War from 1947, discussion of the nuclear option in the US in 2005, Soviet false alarms in 1983). I guess with this being a warning system, it makes sense that those events would have an effect.

I'm more interested in the redacted reading though. I have no idea what it could be and it's maddening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, the use of "Yehom" in 1348 is a reference to this SCP. I was against unnecessarily duplicating technologically advanced Abrahamic Pleistocene civilizations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love SCPs that have to do with recordings or transmissions and this is now one of my favourites; enthusiastic +1

Also, I was wondering: is "Yehom te ehal" a reference to SCP-1348, or is the use of "Yehom" here just a coincidence?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Think about it; if they make their freaking weather balloons self-destruct with nukes when threatened to be intercepted, imagine what their more advanced tech might have done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the work put into that unknown message, feels very real to me. +1, but the "amnesiac" term could be replaced by the one in current use right now.

The way i see it, it is a warning system related to climate change and nuclear events, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I deluded in thinking the title is a Rush reference? If not, why am I the first person to get it? :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man, mysterious ancient languages really grab me, and you did a great job constructing a sample of one that feels ancient. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, if you're hearing the voice of a member of a long-dead civilization that was completely unknown until now, that would probably be worth noting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is that relevant?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Awesome SCP. The only thing that bothers me is that there's no mentioned of what kind of voice reads the broadcasted message; male or female, natural or synthesised. I think that would be worth mentioning.

EDIT: Apparently it was there all along. I don't know how I missed that, I feel a little nuts now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The short answer to your questions is that so many things are left unsaid in the article because there's no way the Foundation can know all the answers to who 1115's builders were and what their motivation was for making it the way they did. It's something they've been studying almost for as long as the Foundation has existed and they still only know bits and pieces of the story. I've elucidated in the past on the greater backstory behind the origin of the drones - find the thread in the forum called "The Leak" if you're interested in knowing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright…I'm not saying this is bad, but how is this so far over 100? And what are people talking about this being their favorite SCP article?! Until the second addendum, all I could think the whole way through was "Get to the point already! What's supposed to be so exceptionally interesting about this?" It's a still-functioning-relic-from-an-ancient-advanced-civilization-we-never-knew-about scip, far from the only one, and it could just as easily be alien scouting machines, and they don't really do anything interesting. What's so amazing about this?

As something requiring Special Containment Procedures, it works, totally, that has the SCP Nazi stamp of approval, but I didn't really get why it even met that basic qualification until after about eight long paragraphs - as an entertainment piece, the hook is just far too late in coming. The second addendum was the only really interesting part. Though I must say, "Yehom te ehel" has a nice ring to it…my brain really likes it for some reason. I don't know, it just keeps repeating in my head in an automated-sounding voice…Ahem, anyway, having read the comments section, I have a new criticism: If the point of the manufacturing date being so ridiculously long ago is that all traces of this civilization would have completely rotted away by now, how is it that they managed to create this one sort of machine that has not deteriorated at all, but nothing else they made still exists? You'd think, if they could make stuff that would last to the present day, they'd make all their stuff that way, or at least as much as possible, not just the (many numerous copies of) one particular device. And sheesh, can you say too many defense mechanisms? EMP blasts and nuclear self-destruct charges? Were the people who made this expecting a completely different civilization to try to interfere with it at some point? And why would they put so much defense on a simple weather monitor anyway? There are pieces of a good article here, but none of them are really anything particularly new, and they aren't put together in a completely cohesive whole, there are cracks and gaps (and not the kinds that are good for a scip).

Bottom line, it's not bad, but it's nothing really exceptional, and the majority of it is rather dull. I will admit that at the time of typing this, I haven't slept in about 36 hours, so I may just be somewhat dimwitted right now…Don't get me wrong, this has my +1, but I'm not rushing to mash the upvote button because "OMG IT'S SO AMAZING AAAAAAAAH!" It's just okay. On the good side of okay, but okay, not amazing. (This is, of course, my own personal opinion, not a statement of absolute fact.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this entry but have to agree on the picture - to me, it's too similar to modern aircraft design to seem older than a century, let alone millions of years.

Maybe a picture of an ancient UFO-like object, or the damaged drone in the museum would work better? Some examples, I'm not demanding you use these: (1 2 3)
I really like how it had a full history of the old Foundation finding it and recovering it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was kind of spoiled for me since I read your post on The Leak first, but it was still really nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Even in an intentionaly obtuse system (messages coded to prevent enemy eavesdropping), a counting system makes for an easily spotted pattern. This is why numbers are usually the first thing deciphered, long before the text content of messages can be understood. Counting up just makes it all the simpler, that's the sort of thing they put on deep space probes to be sure that aliens can figure out what we are trying to communicate.

If the probes used a number system where every single digit had its own unique name, with no relationship to any other number before or after (1,2,3,4…->green, wombat, canyon, ambivalent…), then the experts could be excused for not spotting it early on. But such a code is literally impossible to crack, and that's obviously not the case here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're assuming their number system works the same as ours, being read from one side to the other and strictly increasing by value. Human numbering systems, let alone alien ones, are often much more complicated. Just look at French with it's "four-twenty and eleven" to encode 91, or Roman Numerals where the location of the changing digits varies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The other years I have absolutely no idea about. I've googled them and come up empty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I take it(and I may be wrong) is that those are somehow related to major nuclear events in history. For instance, 1945 is when the atomic bombs were dropped on Japan, 1979 is when Three Mile Island happened, and 1983 had a nuclear false alarm in Russia…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In recordings in 1945, 1947-1973, 1979, 1983, 1997-1998, 2005, and 2009-2011, the phrase "Bé-tevàs" is heard instead.

Trying to work out the significance of these particular years is driving me completely crazy. Can anyone give me a hint?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They're very small, they fly at very high altitude, their surfaces are non-reflective, and the areas they travel over tend to be sparsely populated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A nice SCP, upvoted. But I don't understand how this huge flock of drones could fly around the world every day in broad daylight and still remain unknown to the general public. I'm probably just not reading this right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's nothing particularly significant about the date. The idea I had for this is that it's the last remnant of a pre-human civilization that evolved on Earth and then vanished. The date just needed to be long enough ago that (almost) every relic of that civilization would have decayed into nothingness by now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 12.3 million years… *googles* Serravallian stage, or Meiocene epoch, or Neogene system. … Some minor simple life went extinct. Other than that, nope, don't see it. Is there a significance to the date it was created I'm not seeing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. This may be one of my favorites SCPs ever. Rock on. Don't put the captured one on "Ultra-Fine".

item.title: Re: Smapti
item.content:encoded:
I can't really share in Echo's existential rage here, but I have to agree that the picture looks like something constructed by humans in the last few decades or so. Which it is.

I saw it and thought "weird Soviet probe", which breaks suspension of disbelief. (Although it's not actually a Soviet probe, per E4D on chat. Still though.)

item.title: Re: Smapti
item.content:encoded: … yeah, in this case, basically.

item.title: Re: Smapti
item.content:encoded: I think what you mean to say is: "Don't use a picture." Correct?

item.title: Re: Smapti
item.content:encoded: So, maybe not use a picture of an existing UAV?

item.title: Re: Smapti
item.content:encoded: I would agree that the article would be better not having a picture than having that one. It does break immersion to see something that…look, I don't know shit about UAVs, but that looks like something Lockheed would turn out.

item.title: Re: Smapti
item.content:encoded:
Why in Satan's glorious name would you use a picture of an existing UAV?

Because pictures of non-existing UAVs are surprisingly difficult to come by?

item.title: Re: Smapti
item.content:encoded: I have no idea what kind of expectations you have for the majority of the SCP-wiki users, but I sincerely doubt many of them would be able to identify that UAV off the top of their head.

item.title: Smapti
item.content:encoded:
I hope you lose your fingers to Rottweiler mauling.

Why in Satan's glorious name would you use a picture of an existing UAV? It's the same as taking a picture of a 2005 Chevy Blazer and featuring it in an article about ancient SUVs. Come the fuck on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Judging from the size of the number, these things have been broadcasting for over 1 million years, which is only 1/10th of their current age. The question is what made them start broadcasting?

I'm upvoting

item.title: Extinct civilization or.....
item.content:encoded: +1. broke every mouse at work through the sheer force of my upvote. Gives me the real heeby-jeebies thinking about hte possibilites: Is it an extinct civilization? Are they Extra-Planar? Extra-Temporal? Extra-Terrestrial just waiting for conditions to be JUST right? I dunno…but it creeps me out none the less.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All those UFOs are just weather balloons, eh? Definitely +1, very well written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn Smapti, you're like an SCP factory. Great work, as usual +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It turned out to be remarkably difficult to find a picture of a small UAV that didn't look like an airplane or helicopter and didn't have some guy standing in front of it.

I think the Cypher looks just exotic enough to pass as the last relic of an extinct civilization.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yehom té ehal, Smapti! Yehad ke raznosh? Alhalla behud +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it. I don't know if it's the way it's written, or the story itself, (probably both), but this makes me want to read more. Which is the best thing an SCP article can do. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well written, particularly with regards to the history. It provides just the right amount of information to intrigue but not give away anything. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Trivia: Snake uses one as a single-use jetpack in Smash Bros.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This leaves JUST enough mystery behind that it makes me want to ravenously devour all remaining information. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is excellent! +++++1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the best articles I've read recently, if not ever. I cannot upvote hard enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sikorsky_Cypher circa early 90s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now I want to know everything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The picture looks like a UFO from a low-budget movie circa 1977. Otherwise, this is really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: if a group of linguists can't pick up on a steadily incrementing number sequence in a fixed place in a recurring broadcast with over 30,000 samples… they're fired.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Creepy on a very "my god, we're not masters of this universe?" level. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn Smapti, you just crank out the hits dontcha? +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you should make it clear that the explosion was a form of weaponized self-destruct, since when I first read that I thought the drone had survived the point-blank nuclear explosion.

Good point. Adjusted.

If there was a number that increased by one per broadcast which was always in the same position in the broadcast I would think they'd have been able to decipher the numbering system well before 20██

They'd have to recognize that it was a number at first, which would be hard enough even if it weren't in base-12. I wanted the story of the translation to have a "Rosetta stone" sort of moment - I think the realization that a phrase changes suddenly when man-made radioactivity is first introduced into the atmosphere works nicely for that.
